page: Google Cloud Platform n4a-standard-4 is a general-purpose virtual machine instance belonging to the n4a server family. It is equipped with 4 dedicated vCPUs based on the x86_64 CPU architecture and 16.0 GB of system memory, yielding a balanced allocation of 4.0 GB of RAM per core. This instance does not include local storage or integrated GPUs, and it is provisioned with zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

The hardware profile is designed for balanced resource density, offering a standard 1:4 ratio of vCPUs to memory. This configuration supports general-purpose workloads such as web serving, containerized microservices, small to medium databases, and development environments that require dedicated compute resources without the need for specialized hardware accelerators or local NVMe storage.
description: Google Cloud Platform n4a-standard-4 is a general-purpose virtual machine instance featuring 4 dedicated vCPUs on the x86_64 architecture and 16.0 GB of system memory. This configuration provides a balanced resource density of 4.0 GB of RAM per core, making it suitable for standard workloads that do not require specialized hardware. The instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, and it comes with zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. With its dedicated CPU allocation, the n4a-standard-4 offers predictable compute performance for general-purpose applications. It is well-suited for hosting web servers, running containerized microservices, managing small databases, and supporting development or testing environments.
og_description: Google Cloud Platform n4a-standard-4 is a general-purpose virtual machine featuring 4 dedicated x86_64 vCPUs, 16.0 GB of RAM, and no local storage, designed for balanced workloads.

tagline: A balanced general-purpose virtual machine featuring dedicated compute resources and a standard memory-to-core ratio for everyday workloads.
bullet_points: 16.0 GB of system memory (4.0 GB per core)
No bundled local storage or GPU accelerators
Suitable for web serving, microservices, and small databases
